title: Going Back to Fetch What Was Forgotten

paragraphs: Among the Akan peoples of what is now Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire, there is an old visual symbol — one of the adinkra ideograms used in fabric printing, in goldweights, in carved wooden stools — depicting a bird with its body facing forward and its head turned backward, an egg held in its open beak. The bird is in the act of going forward while reaching back. The egg in the beak is something the bird had left behind and is now retrieving. The ideogram is centuries old, and the proverb it illustrates, spoken in Twi, is among the most cited pieces of practical philosophy in the Akan tradition. The injunction it makes is one Western thought has, until recently, often resisted.

Sankofa is the Akan name for this ideogram and for the principle it expresses. The word is composed of three elements — 'san' (return), 'ko' (go), and 'fa' (take) — that together mean approximately 'go back and fetch.' The full proverb in Twi is sometimes given as 'Se wo were fi na wosankofa a yenkyi' — 'It is not taboo to go back and retrieve what you have forgotten.' The point is moral, not merely practical. The proverb does not say that one ought to go back when convenient; it explicitly removes the cultural prohibition that might otherwise have prevented the return. To go back and fetch what was forgotten is permitted. To go back and fetch what was forgotten is sometimes required. The bird with its head turned, moving forward while reaching back, is a model of the relation to the past that the Akan tradition has worked out across many generations.

What is distinctive about sankofa, as a piece of philosophy, is its relationship to time. The Western traditions of historical thinking that emerged in the nineteenth century — Hegel's progress of spirit, the various Whig and Marxist accounts of historical development — tended to organise time as a directional movement in which the past was progressively superseded by the present and the future. The Akan ideogram proposes a different temporal geometry: the past is not a region one has left but a region one can return to in order to retrieve resources whose absence in the present is causing difficulty. The bird's forward motion does not stop while it reaches back. The future remains the direction of travel. But the retrieval is a continuous operation rather than an episode.

The political appropriation of sankofa in the late twentieth century — particularly in the African-American educational and cultural movements that took up the symbol in the 1960s and 1970s — has at times produced a version of the principle that the original Akan tradition might not entirely recognise. The Pan-African use of sankofa has tended to emphasise the recovery of what colonialism erased: the names, the practices, the genealogies, the languages that were systematically destroyed by the slave trade and by colonial administration. This use is consistent with the original principle but narrower than it. The Akan tradition, in its native context, does not limit sankofa to the recovery of what others have destroyed; it includes the recovery of what one has simply forgotten, what has fallen out of practice, what was not destroyed by anyone but has nonetheless become unavailable. The political use has tended to obscure the more general principle by concentrating on its most urgent contemporary application.

What this opens up, considered more generally, is a question about the relationship between forgetting and progress. The implicit Western assumption — that progress requires the willing abandonment of the past, that the new is built on the cleared ground of the old — has produced a culture remarkably committed to the active forgetting of its own resources. Each generation discards what the previous generation had learned with considerable effort, on the grounds that the new generation has different and presumably better concerns. The Akan principle suggests that this discard is sometimes a real loss, that the past contained resources the present cannot easily reproduce, and that the appropriate response is not to refuse the forward motion but to keep the head turned, the beak open, the willingness to return when the return is needed.

What resists in the popular Western reception of sankofa is the temptation to convert it into either a piece of conservative nostalgia (everything was better in the old days) or a piece of progressive identity politics (everything destroyed must be recovered). The Akan principle is neither. It is a working philosophy of historical attention: the past as a continuously available resource that one is permitted, sometimes required, to return to for what one is currently missing, without giving up the forward motion. The bird with the turned head and the egg in its beak is not a melancholy figure. The bird is in active flight. The bird is simply unwilling to leave the egg behind.
essay.blockquote: The bird is not in mourning — it is in active flight, simply unwilling to leave the egg behind.

positions: Sankofa names a genuine and underexamined relation to the past — the past as a continuously available resource rather than as a superseded region — and the principle is a substantial corrective to the dominant Western progressivism that has organised the past two centuries of European-American thought. To take sankofa seriously is to challenge the assumption that historical progress requires the abandonment of inherited resources, an assumption that has produced real impoverishment.

The contemporary deployment of sankofa, particularly in Anglo-American cultural and educational contexts, has at times become a kind of rhetorical shorthand that flattens the specific Akan philosophical tradition into a slogan for selective historical recovery. The principle's genuine working in Akan thought is more demanding and more nuanced than the popular use suggests, and the appropriation has often borrowed the symbol without the surrounding intellectual architecture that gave it its meaning.

number.label: Approximate year in which the British colonial administration in the Gold Coast, present-day Ghana, first attempted to systematically suppress the production and use of adinkra cloth and its associated symbols — including the sankofa ideogram — as part of a broader policy of replacing local material culture with imported British textiles, a suppression that proved only partially effective and from which the adinkra tradition has continuously recovered over the subsequent century and a half.

fragment: Her grandmother had been a midwife in a village she had not visited in forty years. The recipes for the teas had been in her grandmother's head and her grandmother had been dead for nineteen of those years. She had inherited the kitchen but not the knowledge. She had told herself, for most of her adult life, that the knowledge was unrecoverable. She had finally, that summer, gone back to the village and sat for three weeks with two of the women who had worked alongside her grandmother and who were now in their late eighties. The recipes she came home with were not exactly what her grandmother had used. They were close enough.

word.origin: Twi (Asante Twi), a member of the Akan branch of the Kwa languages of the Niger-Congo family, spoken by approximately ten million people across much of present-day Ghana and parts of Côte d'Ivoire. The word is a compound of three elements: 'san' (to return), 'ko' (to go), and 'fa' (to take or fetch), together meaning 'go back and fetch.' The principle is illustrated by an adinkra ideogram — the bird with its head turned backward, an egg in its open beak — that is among the most widely-circulated symbols in the visual tradition of the Akan peoples. The Pan-African appropriation of the symbol in the late twentieth century has extended its global circulation considerably beyond its original cultural context.
word.definition: The principle that it is permitted, and sometimes required, to return to the past in order to retrieve resources whose absence is causing difficulty in the present — to keep moving forward while reaching back to fetch what was forgotten. The Akan philosophical injunction that historical recovery is not regression but a continuous operation of an organism in motion, distinct from both nostalgia (which would stop the forward motion) and progressivism (which would refuse the return).

quote.text: Se wo were fi na wosankofa a yenkyi.
quote.translation: It is not taboo to return and retrieve what you have forgotten.
quote.author: Akan proverb
quote.source: Traditional Twi proverb, recorded in J.G. Christaller's 'Twi Mmebusɛm Mpensa-Ahansia Mmoaano' (Three Thousand Six Hundred Twi Proverbs in Use Among the Negroes of the Gold Coast), Basel, 1879

on_this_day.text: On 23 June 1956, Kwame Nkrumah's Convention People's Party won a decisive victory in the final pre-independence elections of the British Gold Coast, securing the political mandate that would lead, eight months later on 6 March 1957, to Ghana becoming the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence from European colonial rule. The Ghanaian independence movement, which Nkrumah had led from his return to the country in 1947, was substantially built on a recovery of Akan and broader West African political and cultural traditions that the colonial period had systematically suppressed — including the deliberate revival of adinkra symbols, the formal recognition of the chieftaincy system, and the cultural project that would later be carried forward under the broader Pan-African banner. The sankofa principle in its political register was operating, in the run-up to the elections, as one of the organising rhetorical resources of the movement that was about to take state power.

person.name: Efua Sutherland
paragraphs: Efua Sutherland was the Ghanaian playwright, educator, and cultural activist whose work over the four decades after Ghanaian independence in 1957 substantially founded the modern Ghanaian theatre tradition and produced some of the most-performed African plays of the post-independence period. Her best-known works — 'Edufa' (1962), 'Foriwa' (1962), and 'The Marriage of Anansewa' (1975) — drew on the Akan dilemma-tale tradition and the storytelling form known as Anansesem to produce stage works that operated simultaneously in Twi-inflected English and in the rhythms of older oral performance. She founded the Ghana Drama Studio in 1961, the Kodzidan Storytelling Theatre in the village of Atwia in 1968, and the African Theatre Programme at the University of Ghana, each of which trained a generation of Ghanaian theatre practitioners.

Sutherland's complication is that the cultural-recovery project she helped lead has had a more complicated afterlife than its early ambition suggested. The Ghanaian state has, across the subsequent decades, both celebrated her work and substantially underfunded the theatre institutions she founded; the Kodzidan in particular, the village storytelling theatre that was perhaps her most original institutional contribution, has had only intermittent operation since her death in 1996. The contemporary Ghanaian theatre and storytelling culture is recognisably her descendant, but the systematic continuity of practice that she had envisioned has not been entirely realised — a familiar pattern in which a foundational figure's vision exceeds what the surrounding political conditions can sustain across multiple political cycles.

now_here.name: Cape Coast Castle
now_here.text: A coastal fortress on the Atlantic shore of central Ghana, originally built by the Swedes in 1653, taken by the Danes and then by the Dutch, and from 1664 held by the British, who used it for two centuries as one of the principal embarkation points of the trans-Atlantic slave trade. The castle's dungeons held captive Africans, sometimes for months, before they were marched through the 'Door of No Return' into the holds of the ships that would carry them to the Americas. The site is now a museum administered by the Ghanaian government, and it is one of the principal pilgrimage destinations for African-Americans and other descendants of the enslaved who travel to West Africa as part of the recovery the symbol of sankofa has come to represent in the diaspora. The exit door has, in recent decades, been re-inscribed on its inner side as the 'Door of Return,' marking the symbolic reversal that the Ghanaian state has formally recognised through its 'Year of Return' programme of 2019 and the continuing diaspora policies that have followed it.

questions.text: Is there something you have forgotten — a practice, a piece of knowledge, a relationship — that you sense would still be available for retrieval if you were willing to go back and fetch it?
questions.deeper: What has kept you from the retrieval to this point — the assumption that returning is regression, the cost of the effort, the fear of what you would find when you arrived? And which of these would survive honest examination?
questions.experiment: Pick one thing you have allowed yourself to forget. Take one small concrete step this week toward its retrieval — a phone call to someone who would remember, a return to a place where the practice was conducted, the location of a book or document you have not opened in years. Notice what becomes available once the first step has been taken.

questions.text: The Akan principle distinguishes sankofa from both nostalgia and progressivism. Where in your own life do you tend to slide into one or the other, and what would it look like to operate in the middle — to keep moving forward while remaining open to retrieval?
questions.deeper: Are there parts of your past you have actively refused to revisit on the grounds that the revisiting would be a backward step? And on what evidence is the categorisation of the past as merely behind you accurate?
questions.experiment: Identify one area of your life in which you have committed to forward motion at the cost of explicit refusal of the past. Try, this week, the experiment of remaining in forward motion while also reaching back — keeping the head turned, in the figure of the ideogram — and notice whether the two postures are as incompatible as the dominant cultural assumption suggests.

questions.text: Are there resources in your family, your community, or your tradition that have fallen out of practice and that you suspect would still be available for retrieval if anyone undertook the work?
questions.deeper: Is the work yours to undertake, or does it belong to someone else who has not stepped forward — and if the latter, is the absence of the other person's initiative a sufficient reason for your own non-action? And what would it cost to take responsibility for retrievals that nobody explicitly assigned to you?
questions.experiment: Identify one such resource. Spend an hour this week asking honestly whether you are the appropriate person to undertake the retrieval, and if so, what the first concrete step would look like. Make a deliberate decision either to undertake the work or to release it, rather than continuing in the default state of vague unease about its accumulating disappearance.

trivia.question: The sankofa symbol is one of the most widely-recognised members of which broader West African visual vocabulary, used historically by the Akan peoples in textiles, goldweights, carved stools, and other material objects?
trivia.explanation: Adinkra is the body of visual symbols, ideograms, and motifs used by the Akan peoples of Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire across several centuries of textile, sculpture, and material culture. The symbols are stamped onto cloth using carved calabash dies and natural dyes; each symbol carries a specific proverbial or philosophical meaning, and the combination of symbols on a piece of cloth produces a layered statement that the wearer is making visible. There are between several dozen and several hundred recognised adinkra symbols depending on the catalogue consulted, with sankofa among the most widely known. Nsibidi is a related symbolic system used by Igbo-speaking peoples of southeastern Nigeria; kente is the specific narrow-strip woven cloth tradition of the Akan, distinct from but often combined with adinkra; Mandé is a broader cultural-linguistic designation.

data.prompt: Which of these claims about the sankofa symbol and the broader adinkra tradition is the lie?

statements.text: The sankofa symbol has two principal visual forms in the adinkra tradition: the bird with its head turned backward and an egg in its beak, and a stylised heart-shaped form sometimes called the 'sankofa heart.'
The adinkra symbols were traditionally stamped onto cloth using dies carved from calabash and a dye made from the bark of the Badie tree.

The sankofa principle was first systematically articulated by the Ghanaian philosopher Kwasi Wiredu in a 1974 academic essay; the earlier adinkra symbol did not carry the philosophical meaning attributed to it today.

data.explanation: The lie is the third statement. The sankofa symbol and its associated proverb are documented in pre-colonial Akan oral tradition and were recorded by European observers, including the missionary J.G. Christaller, in the second half of the nineteenth century — well before any modern academic philosopher wrote about them. Kwasi Wiredu is a real and distinguished Ghanaian philosopher, but the sankofa principle was not invented in twentieth-century academic philosophy. The other two statements are accurate: there are indeed two principal visual forms of the symbol, and adinkra cloth was traditionally stamped using calabash dies and bark-based dye.

movie.title: Sankofa
movie.text: The Ethiopian-American director Haile Gerima's feature, made over several years on a small budget and distributed largely through community screenings in the African diaspora, follows a contemporary African-American fashion model on a shoot at Cape Coast Castle who is transported into the past as Shola, an enslaved woman on a sugar plantation. The film's frame — the contemporary descendant pulled back into the historical experience — is itself a working through of the sankofa principle in cinematic form, and the film's distribution history, conducted largely outside the conventional commercial system, has become part of its meaning in the cultures it has reached.
